title: How Rocks Record the Story of Earth

lesson.learn:
Rocks are historical documents. Their layers, minerals, fossils, cracks, and chemical signatures record the conditions under which they formed. Sedimentary layers can show ancient seas, rivers, deserts, or floodplains. Metamorphic rocks reveal pressure and heat. Igneous rocks preserve volcanic or deep crustal cooling. Put together, they let geologists reconstruct Earth's changing surface.

The trick is learning to read the clues in the right order. A rock is not just something solid under our feet. It is evidence.
lesson.simpler: Rocks preserve clues about ancient environments, because their layers and minerals formed under specific conditions.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Geology turns rocks into a record of time.
lesson.deeperDive:
The simplest clue is superposition: in undisturbed sedimentary layers, the oldest layers are usually at the bottom. Fossils add more information by showing which organisms lived when the layer formed. Chemical clues can reveal past oceans, climates, and volcanic events. Radiometric dating then gives those layers an actual age, turning relative history into a timeline.

This is why geology is part detective work and part time machine. Each outcrop is a page, and the whole planet is the book.
lesson.funFact: A rock layer can be older than the mountains above it.
references: USGS - rock layers and geologic time

places: Sedimentary basins - where layers build up
Fossil beds - places where life is preserved
Cliff sections - natural cross-sections of time
years: Present day - Earth history is reconstructed layer by layer
concepts: Stratigraphy - the study of rock layers
Superposition - older layers lie beneath younger ones
Fossil - preserved evidence of past life
Radiometric dating - measuring age with isotopes
Outcrop - exposed rock at the surface
